metadata.title: Refocusing democracy: the Chinese government’s framing strategy in political language

abstract: Authoritarian governments talk about democracy frequently. Why do they do so? What does the term democracy mean in an authoritarian political language? This research uses a computer-assisted text analytical approach illustrating how an authoritarian government strategically manipulates the discourse about democracy to benefit itself. By analysing over a million political articles published in People's Daily over five decades, the study shows a refocusing framing strategy, in which the Chinese government defines democracy not with regime justification but with national policy priorities while denoting democracy most frequently alongside the fundamental values. With this strategy, the framed discourse appear to be consistent with the fundamental values in Western democracies but work for the preservation of the authoritarian regime.

sections.paper_id: Hu - 2020 - Refocusing democracy the Chinese government’s framing strategy in political language
paragraphs: be an intentional result of the governments' manipulation of political language. 5 Nonetheless, few studies show the exact strategies of the governmental manipulations and how they help reconcile the natural conflicts between the established authoritarian regime and popular liberal understanding of democracy. Empirically, while there have been content analyses of authoritarian political languages based on selected articles over limited time periods, long-period full-discourse analyses about democracy are still rare.

This research fills these gaps by examining the manipulation strategy of the Chinese government through the discourse about democracy in a large political language corpus. The discourse is defined as the official political language discussing or mentioning democracy. The research argues that the Chinese government applied a delicate strategy refocusing the discourse from what democracy is to what the government is doing for democracy in favour of the established regime. The strategy allows the government to avoid a direct, harsh justification for the regime and reconcile the conflict between the prevalent liberal perception of democracy and the established authoritarian regime.

Utilizing computer-assisted text analysis together with network analysis and econometric inferences, this research examines this argument together with two alternatives theories (the universal-value and justification strategies) in a corpus of over a million political articles published over five decades. The results show that, in contrast to the prevailing views, the Chinese government frames the concept of democracy not directly to justify the authoritarian regime. Nor does it frame the term based on normative liberal values. Rather, the definition of democracy is most closely associated with national policy priorities while the discourse about democracy as a whole is set to appear with the discourse about liberal values of democracy most frequently through the entire corpus. These findings offer a new insight of how an authoritarian government can utilize the discourse about democracy to serve for the regime preservation in an age of democratization with potential implications for understanding the resilience of authoritarian regimes more generally.

sections.paper_id: Hu - 2020 - Refocusing democracy the Chinese government’s framing strategy in political language
sections.header_raw: Political language manipulation and mass democracy perception in China
sections.header_norm: political language manipulation and mass democracy perception in china
paragraphs: Promoting liberal democracy that built upon the theory and political practices of Western democratic countries has already become a global norm. 6 According to the Polity IV index of 2015, over half of the countries in the world have adopted such regime to some extent (above 6 in the institutionalized democracy score). The remaining authoritarian governments thus face challenges not only from coping with economic and political competitions with democratic countries but also in sustaining legitimacy domestically under the increasing global pressure of democratization.

Authoritarian governments have been well documented of taking up the democracies' challenges through economic development, 7 social mobility 8 and domestic and foreign policies. 9 In contrast, scholars of political communication argue that language and media are also critical tools. 10 The authorities can use them against the democratization pressure by at least two ways: controlling for dissent information and framing authoritarian-friendly perceptions.

The study of information control has boomed recently by taking advantage of the development of political methodology and data science. Scholars utilize cutting-edge methods and novel sources to gather large amounts of qualitative and quantitative data and use them to illustrate how authoritarian governments control the intensity and direction of sensitive topics through censorship and distractions. 11 Comparatively, the research on how authoritarian governments frame political language and steer public perceptions is still in development. On the one hand, scholars have witnessed the consistent endeavours of authoritarian governments all over the world to shape political discourses. 12 In China, in particular, studies have demonstrated that the authority has continually made such efforts throughout the periods from the wartime to the post-Mao era. 13 In terms of democracy, despite the controversies at the elite level regarding the usage of policy instruments borrowed from liberal democracies, the effort of shaping the perception of democracy at the mass level has never been stopped. 14 Correspondingly, Chinese citizens have shown distinctive understanding of democracy from those in the Western world, such as expecting more guardianship components and focusing more on the utility rather than the process of democracy. 15 On the other hand, our understanding of how this democracy perception was achieved is not complete yet. Still taking the Chinese case, although scholars tend to relate this "Chinese style" of democracy perception with a variety of factors, such as characteristics of the established regime, 16 legacies of Chinese political culture/ history, 17 and under-developed civil society and liberal culture in the contemporary Chinese society, 18 few studies explain exactly how or through what these factors make the influences. A common "taken as granted" assumption attributes the public perception of democracy to the governments' political framing. However, there is little empirical evidence about this. We do not know to what extent the mass perception reflects the government's framing of the democracy discourse. Nor do we even have a comprehensive view of how democracy is defined and what discourses it relates to in the contemporary Chinese political language.

Furthermore, despite the information control, Chinese people still have opportunities to access the information about the liberal democratic world. 19 Studies have also found that Chinese people are not against the liberal values that Western democracy is based on. 20 In this sense, how would the Chinese government maintain the public democracy perception as the authoritarian-friendly version, when its citizens are aware of what liberal democracy is and how it runs?

This study seeks the answers to these questions by examining the framing strategy of the Chinese authority on the discourse of democracy through a full-discourse perspective. While acknowledging the variance of the official narratives of democracy under the different socioeconomic and political conditions, 21 this research focuses on the relatively stable components in the concept that the authority can use to reinforce the regime legitimization and maintain resilience against the ideological challenge from the Western democracies. 22 The following sections first provide three models of democracy framing and then examine them within a half-a-century corpus of Chinese political language.

Three strategies of discourse framing I use two dimensions to identify the potential strategies of manipulation democracy discourse that an authoritarian government can use. The first dimension is the liberalism nature in conceptualizing democracy. The omnipresence of liberal democracy in global discourse makes modern authoritarian governments increasingly difficult to completely ignore the concept of democracy in their political language. When incorporating the concept, a government needs to decide how they would adopt this popular definition of the concept. The second dimension is the coherence with the established polity. Given the ultimate goal of incorporating democracy is to sustain the legitimacy of the authoritarian regime, the governments need to decide to what extent they would manipulate the concept to reflect or be coherent with the characteristics of the political institutions, policies, and wills of the established regime. 23 In this two-dimension system, I identify three potential strategies, as shown in Figure 1. They lead the government's efforts to different directions in manipulating the democracy discourse in the political language both in terms of defining the concept at the within-discourse level and connecting with other conceptual discourses at the cross-discourse level. At the upper left corner is the universal-value strategy by which an authoritarian government recognizes the universality of liberal democracy and adopts its definition. It results in a discourse of democracy that is highly coherent with liberal democratic values (such as freedom and equality) and high contradictory to the authoritarian nature of the established regime. At the cross-discourse level, the discourse is expected to appear most frequently alongside the discourse about democratic liberal values due to the conceptual coherence. 24 The universal-value strategy involves largely no language manipulation. Few authoritarian governments would fully adopt it unless in an ongoing regime transition. 25 Yet, it serves as a clear theoretical baseline in comparison with the following two alternatives.

The justification strategy at the lower right corner is the opposite of the universalvalue strategy, a strategy that the common wisdom often suggests. The strategy contends that the government has an intention and sufficient power to redefine democracy with the characteristics of the authoritarian regime in favor of the regime's legitimacy. 26 The strategy can be implemented by discrediting and criticizing the liberal democracy and/or arguing the superior validity and efficiency of the established (authoritarian) regime in terms of reflecting people's wills and achieving a long-term development of the country. As a result, the manipulated definition of democracy is expected to be highly coherent with the authoritarian characteristics of the regime with little account for the conflict with the popular liberal definition. At the cross-discourse level, the discourse about democracy should closely associate with the discourse about regime characteristics rather than liberal values.

Finally, the refocusing strategy at the center of the system presents a middle way between simply adopting the liberal democracy and completely redefining the concept with authoritarian features. The strategy suggests not to deny the liberal definition of democracy normatively but to refocus the definition from what it is to what the government is doing for democracy. To do so, the national policy priorities that the government is working on in a given time span are used to define democracy. Such definition emphasizes the plans and efforts for democracy instead of the principles and goals of democracy. This perspective switching prevents a direct battle with the popular liberal understanding of democracy. The authoritarian government's efforts to promote national development and power maintenance become contributions to democracy. Achievements in national policies are thus not only the source of the authoritarian regime's legitimacy but also the evidence of the government's positive attitude towards democracy and active pursuit of democratic reforms. 27 Since the conceptual contradiction no longer exists, the government no longer needs to worry about discussing the liberal values. Instead, frequently discussing them together with the democracy discourse may even help convince citizens, especially those who had been exposed to liberal democracy, to accept the manipulated version. As the associative learning principle in psychology indicates, mentioning concepts together will reinforce the audience's cognitive connection between them, regardless whether they were connected initially. 28 The refocusing strategy shares the same ultimate goal as the justification strategy to legitimize the regime but through a more delicate approach that can enhance the persuasive power by connecting with the prevalent values of liberal democracy as the universal-value strategy performs. In practice, the strategy suggests a two-level manipulation: (1) defining the concept with national policy priorities within the democracy discourse, and (2) tying the discourse with the discourse about liberal values at the cross-discourse level.

The following section examines the above three strategies with the real-life data of the contemporary Chinese political language based on a novel approach. The approach combines multiple quantitative analytical methods that can test the inferences of the strategies both within the discourse where the concept is defined and across the discourses relating to other critical discourses.

sections.paper_id: Hu - 2020 - Refocusing democracy the Chinese government’s framing strategy in political language
sections.header_raw: A within-& cross-discourse analysis of democracy discourse
sections.header_norm: a within cross discourse analysis of democracy discourse
paragraphs: To examine the government's strategies of framing democracy requires a bird's eye view of the entire discourse democracy and also its relationships with other discourses over time. This is a task that conventional methods, such as surveys and qualitative readings, are difficult to achieve. While surveys are valuable tools for investigating political language framing, they explore some framing consequences at best rather than measuring the language manipulation directly. The qualitative methods are also helpful yet heavily rely on human coding and subjective comparison. They could be very laborand time-consuming that limits the scope and time frame of the studies. The sample size of such studies is often restricted to a single topic, a specific format (such as editorials), or documents produced during a short time span. It is thus difficult to use this method to detect government's language manipulations across multiple topics over an extended time period.

Computer-assisted text analysis provides a solution to these methodological limits. It can help researchers inquire the underlying strategies of political communication through political texts and has demonstrated its power in the studies of citizen-tocitizen communications. 29 The method utilizes computer power to detect patterns and trends in language and written documents beyond grammar and rhetoric. This article will use this method to examine the three possible strategies in manipulating discourse about democracy in the Chinese political language.

The examination will focus on People's Daily. The newspaper is the core official media of the Communist Party of China (CPC). As the "mouthpiece" of the Central Committee of the Party, it "represent[s] the viewpoint of the Chinese leadership". 30 People's Daily plays a dominant role in the construction of the Chinese political language. The Party leaders regard the newspaper as the Party's main "battlefield" of the construction of political knowledge and ideology and the critical tool to provide the "spiritual power and certainty" to the party and the entire country. 31 To achieve these goals, the Publicity Department will cautiously frame and tightly control every piece of news and commentaries the newspaper contains, in order to present the central's attitudes and opinions accurately. 32 Some pieces even need the top leaders' editing and endorsement in person to be disseminated to the public. 33 For the rest state, semi-state, and commercial media, People's Daily serves as the most authentic source of political vocabulary and syntax and the safest model for the language framing in political reports and commentaries.

Of course, constructing the Chinese political language is not the only goal for People's Daily articles. Tsai and Kao revealed based on in-depth interviews with the newspaper's editors that, in some cases, the articles could serve as a tool for CCP leaders to vent discontent or manifest power in factional struggles. 34 They can also be used by the leaders to send signals to specific government departments or socio-economic actor groups. 35 Despite these performances in the governmentto-government and elite-to-elite communications, they would not diminish the function of the newspaper to shape the mass political perceptions in the government-to-citizen communications. A core task of People's Daily for the latter is to maintain the image of "party unity" and the continuity of the Party's spirit and statements among the people. 36 Therefore, if there is a consistent strategy of political language framing, the People's Daily articles are arguably the most qualified sources to seek for them. This point has also been demonstrated by a growing number of studies that use People's Daily articles as the primary data source. 37 This study follows this line and elevates the investigation of People's Daily to a full-discourse level.

For this research, I collected all the 1,371,607 articles published in this unique source of Chinese political language during 58 years, covering the important periods from the founding of the communist government to two generations of leadership in the post-Mao era. 38 The approach investigates the political language in this corpus at two levels: the within-discourse level analysis detects how democracy was defined, and the cross-discourse level analysis identifies how the democracy discourse was framed along with other political discourses.
sections.text:
To examine the government's strategies of framing democracy requires a bird's eye view of the entire discourse democracy and also its relationships with other discourses over time. This is a task that conventional methods, such as surveys and qualitative readings, are difficult to achieve. While surveys are valuable tools for investigating political language framing, they explore some framing consequences at best rather than measuring the language manipulation directly. The qualitative methods are also helpful yet heavily rely on human coding and subjective comparison. They could be very laborand time-consuming that limits the scope and time frame of the studies. The sample size of such studies is often restricted to a single topic, a specific format (such as editorials), or documents produced during a short time span. It is thus difficult to use this method to detect government's language manipulations across multiple topics over an extended time period.

Computer-assisted text analysis provides a solution to these methodological limits. It can help researchers inquire the underlying strategies of political communication through political texts and has demonstrated its power in the studies of citizen-tocitizen communications. 29 The method utilizes computer power to detect patterns and trends in language and written documents beyond grammar and rhetoric. This article will use this method to examine the three possible strategies in manipulating discourse about democracy in the Chinese political language.

The examination will focus on People's Daily. The newspaper is the core official media of the Communist Party of China (CPC). As the "mouthpiece" of the Central Committee of the Party, it "represent[s] the viewpoint of the Chinese leadership". 30 People's Daily plays a dominant role in the construction of the Chinese political language. The Party leaders regard the newspaper as the Party's main "battlefield" of the construction of political knowledge and ideology and the critical tool to provide the "spiritual power and certainty" to the party and the entire country. 31 To achieve these goals, the Publicity Department will cautiously frame and tightly control every piece of news and commentaries the newspaper contains, in order to present the central's attitudes and opinions accurately. 32 Some pieces even need the top leaders' editing and endorsement in person to be disseminated to the public. 33 For the rest state, semi-state, and commercial media, People's Daily serves as the most authentic source of political vocabulary and syntax and the safest model for the language framing in political reports and commentaries.

Of course, constructing the Chinese political language is not the only goal for People's Daily articles. Tsai and Kao revealed based on in-depth interviews with the newspaper's editors that, in some cases, the articles could serve as a tool for CCP leaders to vent discontent or manifest power in factional struggles. 34 They can also be used by the leaders to send signals to specific government departments or socio-economic actor groups. 35 Despite these performances in the governmentto-government and elite-to-elite communications, they would not diminish the function of the newspaper to shape the mass political perceptions in the government-to-citizen communications. A core task of People's Daily for the latter is to maintain the image of "party unity" and the continuity of the Party's spirit and statements among the people. 36 Therefore, if there is a consistent strategy of political language framing, the People's Daily articles are arguably the most qualified sources to seek for them. This point has also been demonstrated by a growing number of studies that use People's Daily articles as the primary data source. 37 This study follows this line and elevates the investigation of People's Daily to a full-discourse level.

For this research, I collected all the 1,371,607 articles published in this unique source of Chinese political language during 58 years, covering the important periods from the founding of the communist government to two generations of leadership in the post-Mao era. 38 The approach investigates the political language in this corpus at two levels: the within-discourse level analysis detects how democracy was defined, and the cross-discourse level analysis identifies how the democracy discourse was framed along with other political discourses.

sections.paper_id: Hu - 2020 - Refocusing democracy the Chinese government’s framing strategy in political language
sections.header_raw: A within-discourse analysis of the definition of democracy
sections.header_norm: a within discourse analysis of the definition of democracy
paragraphs: The within-discourse analysis focuses on the definition of democracy within its discourse. The discourse includes all the articles mentioning "democracy" and its synonyms, amounting to 11.2% of the whole corpus. 39 Defining democracy is considered as a model of the association between democracy and other concepts in the democracy discourse. According to the hypotheses, the research particularly focuses on the relationships of democracy with three concepts: normative liberal values, regime characteristics, and national policy priorities. 40 The following section explains how the conceptual association is measured over the thousands of articles of the democracy discourse.

sections.paper_id: Hu - 2020 - Refocusing democracy the Chinese government’s framing strategy in political language
sections.header_raw: Measuring conceptual definitions with topic modelling
sections.header_norm: measuring conceptual definitions with topic modelling
paragraphs: In People's Daily, democracy could be addressed in various contexts. It could be discussed with political governance in rural areas:

The most important part of building a transparent village governance is … to improve the village autonomy led by the Party and build transparent, democratic management and accountability system. … [The Party branches] should also support the Village Committees to promote transparent and democratic governance and protect the rural masses' democratic rights. 41 Or involved in a discussion about individual development and entrepreneurship:

The development of socialist political democracy and political civilization is a critical guarantee of an appropriate circumstance for entrepreneurship. … Following the plan made at the Sixteenth National Congress of the Communist Party of China, we should actively combine the leadership of the Party, the dominance of the People, and the rule of law, so as to improve the socialist democracy further and protect the extensive rights and freedom of the citizens. 42 To investigate how democracy is framed through thousands of such paragraphs over thousands of articles over five decades, I applied a computer-assistant text-analysis method, structural topic modelling (STM). The method allows researchers to identify concepts and their mutual relationships over the entire corpus-all the articles in the democracy discourse, in our case-even if they do not know ahead how the concept is constructed exactly in the texts. 43 Since the method trains the computer to "read" the articles rather, it minimize the potential measurement errors due to inconsistent judgments and mistakes in human coding. However, to allow the computer assistance, researchers have to break the paragraphs and sentences of articles into words, which means ignoring some critical aspects in the original articles, such as the grammars, syntax, context, and more complex rhetoric and ideology orientations.

In this sense, STM would not serve as an ideal tool for studying the detailed framing techniques or dynamics of the political contexts. What the methods like STM are good at is to detect conceptual relationships (e.g. whether the two concepts are identical or different or whether the meaning of them are similar or opposite) beyond articles. Research in language and cultural science have demonstrated the close association between term distribution and conceptual relationships in a topic discourse based on multiple articles. 44 There have been also a growing literature in social science, especially, political science, that utilizes this method to identify conceptual patterns in newspapers, official documents, speeches, media posts, and a variety of political-language corpora. 45 Following this line, I uses STM to identify the concepts and their relationships in the democracy discourse of People's Daily, in order to examine whether the consistent framing strategy of democracy over time.

An important decision for processing STM is the number of concepts (topics) to identify. This decision determines the scale and the connotation of each identified concept. There is no consensus on the "best" topic number of a given corpus or on how to find it. The research conducts a data-driven approach to compare the model performance based on identifying 20-120 topics and found that 40 performed the best. The sensitivity of the initial point was also taken into account. All the candidate models with different initial points went through an expectation-maximization process, and the most reliable model was selected (see more details in Online Appendix II.1). Given the identified concepts, I calculate the proportion of a given document allocated to each topic (θ) and use the correlations of the topic θs to estimate the relationships between concepts with each other by years. 46 The next issue to conquer after the topic identification is topic recognition. As deciding the topic numbers and initial point, there is no consensus on the best way to recognize topics (concepts) from the outcomes of an unsupervised text analysis. I thus use a supervised process to complete the task based on the information from both data and theory-led criteria. The process includes three steps: first, keyword selection through a class-based process. 47 I select three equivalent representative keywords for each topic. 48 Each word is selected following two criteria: (1) how well the selected word can represent the connotation of the concept, and ( 2) to what extent the word's meaning is semantically exclusive. The former criterion involves theoretical classifications of the core meanings of each concept and how these meanings are presented in the Chinese language. The latter criterion aims to avoid words with ambiguous meanings. For instance, the word "enemy" might relate to national security, but it may also relate to targets in class struggles, political opponents, or insects in agriculture. These meanings are not exclusive and clear, and the word would thus not be used as the keyword to identify the topic. Table 1 gives some examples of the selected keywords, and the full list and more technical details are in Online Appendix I. The second step is to identify how important each keyword is to the given concept. The importance is measured by frequency exclusivity (FREX) scores. FREX "summarizes words with the harmonic means of the probability of appearance under a topic and the exclusivity of that topic". 49 It provides more semantically intuitive information about a word's contribution to the topic than raw frequency counts or other measures, which will increase the accuracy of matching the concept to its substantive meaning. In the third step, the concept is recognized referring to the keyword ranking in FREX. For example, if the keywords of democracy have the highest FREX score in one topic, the topic is thus recognized as representing the concept of democracy. (See more technical details in Online Appendix II.2).

Figure 2 shows the conceptual networks of the democracy discourse in the People's Daily articles of 1990 and 1997, as the outcome examples of the above procedure. The nodes are the concepts, and the edges represent their correlations to each other. The thicker the edge is, the more closely the concepts correlate. Democracy and five other concepts of interest are highlighted. In the network of 1990 when the CCP's political control encountered the challenges both from inside and outside due to the immediate incident, 50 the concept connected to political stability the most. In 1997, the year Hong Kong was returned to the Chinese government, national security became the concept that relating to democracy the most. These connection patterns match the common understanding of the political language in the given years. The variance of connections across the years implies the dynamic nature of the democracy discourse as indicated by the existing literature. Both signs verify the validity of the outcomes based on the computer-assistant measurement.

After recognizing the topic of democracy, the sum of its correlations with all the other topics is used to measure the definition of democracy and the dependent variable in the later econometric analysis. The bilateral associations between democracy and liberal values, regime feature, and national priorities respectively serve as explanatory variables. Their contributions to the variance of the definition of democracy will be used as evidence of the universal-value, justification, and refocusing strategies respectively.

To further adjust for the potential biases due to the ignorance of the article contexts, the examining model includes three types of controls. I first control for characteristics of the conceptual network in the given year based on the measurement of the neighbourhood effect and centrality. The neighbourhood effect refers to the situation in which a strong connection between democracy and another concept because both of them closely connect to a third concept, e.g. a faction in the Party or a socio-economic section. The centrality accounts for the situation when a certain concept became a popular node that relate to all the other important concepts of the given political discourse, for instance, "revolution" in the early years of People's Republic of China and "openness" since 1978. Second, the periodic and time dependency are taken into account, since the content of the newspaper may appear certain features during certain period or year (such as more class-struggle narratives used during "Cultural Revolution" or the attitudinal changes to the U.S. when the government strove for the recovery of the formal diplomatic relationship). Third, I control for the dynamic of the international environment, given the democratization pressure from the Western World and China's resilience vary over time. (See the detailed measurements of these controls in Appendix II.3.).

(See the detailed measurements of these controls in Appendix II.3.).

sections.paper_id: Hu - 2020 - Refocusing democracy the Chinese government’s framing strategy in political language
sections.header_raw: Exploring the conceptual relationships at the cross-discourse level
sections.header_norm: exploring the conceptual relationships at the cross discourse level
paragraphs: After examining the definition of democracy within its discourse, I go beyond the discourse and examine the conceptual relationship over discourses in the entire People's Daily corpus. The justification strategy contents that the frequency of articles about democracy should appear in the newspaper more frequently with articles about regime characteristics. In contrast, the refocusing strategy expects that the articles about democracy should be organized to appear more frequently with articles about liberal values for more persuasive power. 51 These arguments are tested with the yearly count of articles from each discourse of interest based on the frequency of keywords in the articles. 52 The data are fitted to a negative binomial model, where the dependent variable is the counts of keywords in the discourse about democracy, and the explanatory variables are counts of the keywords from other discourses. The temporal and international influences are controlled as in the within-discourse study. An additional control is the size of the newspaper. With the increasing demand for information, the total number of articles in the newspaper changes over time. Newspapers may also publish supplemental issues for special occasions like festivals or the occurrence of important domestic or international events. Such effects are controlled by the total number of articles each year. The information of all the variables relating to the cross-discourse is presented in Online Appendix III.2 for readers' check.

sections.paper_id: Hu - 2020 - Refocusing democracy the Chinese government’s framing strategy in political language
paragraphs: The main empirical results are illustrated in Figure 3, where the dots are the coefficient estimates and whiskers are 95% confidence intervals (also see the numeric results in Online Appendix IV). The left panel represents the results of the within-discourse analysis, and the right shows those of the cross-discourse analysis. All the coefficients in Figure 3 are standardized by dividing by two standard errors before being fitted into the models, and thus comparable. 53 The result of the within-discourse analysis shows national policy priorities are the only aspect significantly affecting the definition of democracy, ceteris paribus. Although the contribution of national security is not different from zero, both economic development and social stability have significantly positive coefficients. Meanwhile, the coefficient of neither regime characteristics nor liberal values is statistically different from zero. In defining democracy in the People's Daily, the only factor that matters is neither the liberal values nor the regime characteristics, but national priorities Ths result is counter to the inferences of both the universal-value strategy and the justification strategy and supports the expectations of the refocusing strategy.

The results of the cross-discourse analysis are also consistent with the refocusing strategy. Articles from the discourse about democracy were published most frequently with articles about liberal values, after controlling for other factors. The association with the regime is negative although insignificantly. For the national priorities, neither economic development nor stability shows a significant effect at the cross-discourse level. As the refocusing strategy predicts, the primary manipulation at the cross-discourse level is to highlight the connection and concordance between the government's refocused democracy and the commonly-used democracy discourse based on the non-authoritarian principles.

The performance of the control variables further demonstrates the consistency of the refocusing strategy. At the within-discourse level, none of the network, temporal, or international power controls significantly affect the definition of democracy. The definition of democracy looks insensitive to any of these contextual factors. I conducted two more conditional-effect models to test if its connection with the national priorities is stable over time and various international environments. The results show that the refocusing strategy keeps consistent regardless of the changes in the periodic and international conditions (see Online Appendix V).

At the cross-discourse level, the only significant contextual variable is newspaper size. It shows that, when the People's Daily publishes more articles, fewer of them relate to the discourse about democracy. This result is not surprising, however. Given the authoritarian nature of the regime, the Chinese authority ought to be more willing to engage in other contents rather than raising more discussions about democracy when there are more rooms in the newspapers.

sections.paper_id: Hu - 2020 - Refocusing democracy the Chinese government’s framing strategy in political language
paragraphs: In this study, I examined the governmental framing strategies of democracy based on a full-discourse survey of all the articles published in People's Daily over a half of a century. The results imply that:

. The Chinese authority has applied a consistent refocusing strategy to frame democracy at both within-and cross-discourse levels over time. . At the within-discourse level, the authority refocuses democracy to national priorities when defining the concept. . At the cross-discourse level, the authority associates the discourses of democracy with the discourse of universal values that liberal democracy is based on closer than with other discourses.

This refocusing strategy reflects the efforts of the Chinese government to take up the ideological challenge from the liberal democratization. With these systematic, deliberate, and enduring efforts, it would not be surprised that Chinese citizens continue to hold the democracy perception that favours the authoritarian regime, even if they have been exposed to how democracy is run out of the country. 54 The findings of the refocusing strategy have far-reaching implications for understanding modern authoritarian propaganda and re-legitimation strategies in general.

In terms of political propaganda, the findings talk to a recently growing literature that points out that non-indoctrinated, subtle propaganda is usually more effective than hard-line propaganda in authoritarian political communications. 55 The refocusing strategy illustrates a specific format of this type of political persuasion. It further shows that this soft, controversy-avoiding style can be applied not only to concrete messages design in government-citizen interactions, but also the giant plan to shape public political perceptions over decades.

In a more general view, the refocusing strategy reflects the salient power of authoritarian governments to deploy their governance and re-legitimation efforts. The deployment can be enduring (applying a consistent strategy over decades) and comprehensive (at both the within-and cross-discourse levels). To investigate such deployments then requires a full-discourse view over the entire political corpus rather than focusing on a limited number of selected political documents.

The novel big-data-based method has demonstrated unique advantages to achieve this goal. The method enables me to survey millions of documents together and discover patterns and trends both over time and across texts, which would be difficult to achieve with conventional methods. I encourage future researchers to apply this method to broader areas of authoritarian politics, such as legislature, judiciary, and foreign affairs for both further examining the generalizability of the findings of this research and discovering new strategies in authoritarian governance and legitimization. Researchers are also encouraged to involve more diverse sources of data, such as additional newspapers, journals, speeches, and online debates. 56 After all, even just for the case of China, the government's political language manipulation rarely engage only one type of media. 57 Meanwhile, researchers should also be clearly aware of the limitations of the big-data based design and methods, especially in terms of the omission of contextual information and functional components from the original texts. In such research the big-data approach based on word or word-group tracing would not be powerful enough to interpret the delicate context and even completely neglect what happened in the text. Researchers should be fully acquainted with this limitation when using the big-data approach and be very cautious the potential traps (such as oversimplification and misinterpretation) the approach might lead to. Moreover, in spite of the competence of the bigdata approach to this study, the unearthed refocusing strategy is by no mean the only strategy practiced in the reality. It can just be a part of a broader, more complex governmental manipulation that waits for future studies to further explore.
